meta.title: Farmer Drop — DR-1 Take-Rate Working Parameters
meta.purpose: Single source of truth for the DR-1 (platform take-%) working analysis. Drives take_rate_math.html. The take-% is UNDECIDED; these are template solutions to decide it, not decided numbers.
audit_items_respected: §3.4.1 100x ambiguity (0.10% vs 10%) is load-bearing — model halts until resolved.
§3.4.2 credit boost is a negative-margin subsidy — must show funding source (breakage / 0.25% wedge / token float).
§3.4.3 double-charging risk — Stripe % passthrough vs. platform's own %; kept explicit.
§3.4.4 PMA 'fees-are-a-tax' is a characterization, not a mechanism — only the token rail actually removes Stripe cost.
§3.4.5 two-hop haircut needs a worked example vs a real incumbent baseline — both columns shown.
§3.4.6 adoption (CAC, drop density) is the binding constraint, not throughput.

sources.label: USDA ERS Food Dollar Series — farm share
sources.note: 2024: farm share 11.8 cents per $ of all domestically produced food; food-at-home farm share 18.5 cents; food-away-from-home 7.1 cents. Farm establishments received ~24.3 cents of each food-at-home dollar in 2023 (primary-factor series).

sources.label: USDA ERS Price Spreads from Farm to Consumer
sources.note: Fresh-produce farm share: basket of 16 fresh vegetables 20–29% since 2010; basket of 13 fresh fruits 32–39%. Retailing margins <25% of retail price for most tracked items.

sources.label: Broadline / produce distributor & retail margins
sources.note: Broadline produce distributors: 10–15% gross margin, 2–5% net; specialty/organic 20–30%. Distributor markup ~18–20% (range 5–40%). Retailers add ~30–50% on distributor cost.

sources.label: CSA / farmers-market farm share
sources.note: Traditional outlets return ~15 cents of the food dollar to farmers; at a farmers market / CSA effectively ~100% of the food dollar stays with the local farmer (no distributor or retail hop).

contractor.note: 3% of a HostDrop's volume, paid OUT of the platform take, for N months. inWindowFraction=1 models bootstrap (every drop in-window); at steady state most volume has aged out and the drag falls toward the fraction of volume in newly-recruited drops.

incumbent.note: representativeGroceryFreshShare (22%) is the conservative midpoint used for the side-by-side 'double the farmer' check; csaFarmShare (~98%) is the direct-channel ceiling. Farmer Drop sits between them.

dr1.note: 0.10% (strawman) is refuted below. Reproducing the map's $5M/wk net and $5.2B valuation requires ~5% NET, which given a 3% contractor drag + 2% credit boost implies a ~10% GROSS take. Any positive growth-capital + opex cushion needs take >= ~7%. Supported range: 7–12%, centered 10%.

templates.name: Flat Ten
templates.oneLiner: 10% flat take. Closes exactly (80 farmer + 10 hops + 10 take = 100) and reproduces the map's $5M/wk net and $5.2B valuation. Credit boost eats into take unless breakage appears.
templates.verdict: Reproduces the stated valuation; farmer 80% (~3.6x incumbent grocery). Weakness: DR-2 credit boost is unfunded and quietly cuts net take to ~5%.

templates.name: Lean Eight
templates.oneLiner: 8% take + 0.25% wedge; 2% credit boost explicitly funded by breakage (~1.5%) + wedge (~0.25%) so it never touches the take (DR-2 closed).
templates.verdict: Cleanest DR-2 story: boost funded off-take, farmer 82%. Smaller growth cushion (~5% net) and depends on breakage materializing (~1.5% of credit float).

templates.name: Tiered / Decaying
templates.oneLiner: 12% during bootstrap (funds CAC + host-drop density) decaying to 7% at scale, with the take falling as a HostDrop's Drop-Score-weighted volume grows. Member story: 'fees fall as the network regenerates.'
templates.verdict: Best growth-capital + member narrative and ties fees to the Drop Score. Costs: two rates to communicate and a governance rule for the decay schedule.
